name: First law of thermodynamics (energy conservation including heat)
principles: energy conservation extended to thermal processes
internal energy as a state function
heat and work as path-dependent energy transfers
conventions: U is the internal energy (state function): depends only on the current macrostate, not the history
delta Q is the heat absorbed BY the system from the surroundings (positive when system gains heat)
delta W is the work done BY the system ON the surroundings (positive when system pushes outward)
delta notation (not d) emphasises Q and W are inexact differentials — path-dependent, not state functions
alternative sign convention used in chemistry: dU = delta Q + delta W with delta W defined as work done ON the system; check the textbook before computing
for a quasi-static, reversible expansion of a fluid: delta W = P dV
expectedLimits: isolated system (delta Q = 0, delta W = 0): dU = 0; internal energy strictly conserved
adiabatic process (delta Q = 0): dU = -delta W; internal energy changes only through work
isobaric process (P const): delta Q = dH where H = U + PV is enthalpy
isochoric process (V const): delta W = 0 (no PV work), so dU = delta Q
isothermal ideal-gas expansion: dU = 0 (ideal gas internal energy depends only on T), so delta Q = delta W
cyclic process: integral of dU around the cycle = 0; net heat absorbed = net work done
references: Clausius (1850) — Über die bewegende Kraft der Wärme
Joule (1845) — mechanical equivalent of heat experiment
Callen — Thermodynamics and an Introduction to Thermostatistics, ch.1-2
